So I want to tell you about donuts. So some new folks often come up and ask me because nothing dies on the web anymore, as you know. So all the Dan fat pictures are still on the web and will be there forevermore. So some people are like surprised that I'm not fatter and younger and have more hair than I do. And so for many years I carried 245 to 255 pounds, give or take. Most of the time I was on the success events, I was about 245. And this is one of the reasons why. Jacket size was 52 to give you a frame of reference. And so this is one of the reasons why. There's a few things in life that I find irresistible. One of which are donuts. And so when we lived in Phoenix for a time, on the street between my house and my office, when I used to go to the office, there was a donut shop, Dunkin' Donuts. And it was conveniently located on the right side of the street. So you didn't have to cross traffic to get to it. Therefore, pretty much, not that that would have stopped me, but it just made it easier. So pretty much every morning I stopped just to be a nice guy and get some donuts for the office, most of which went into this office, thereby helping maintain a 245 to 250 pound weight. The solution to that was I sold a house and we moved. So it has not to pass the donut shop on the way to the office. So if anybody in here in their normal life, in their day job, is in real estate, there's all sorts of reasons to list and sell a house. And there's one of them. It's on the route where there's a donut shop. And so I have the iron will not to go get any, as long as I'm not passing any. But I do not have the iron will not to eat any. And I am aware of that about myself. I occasionally now get salad at Panera Bread. And they, because they call themselves Panera Bread, not Panera Salad, they feel compelled to put a small loaf of bread in with every salad. So if you buy two salads to take home, you get two little loaves of bread. And because I understand myself very well in the garage, before I come into the house, I take the bread out of the bag and I rip it into parts and I put it in the garbage, deep in the garbage, so that I do not go back out there and get it out of the garbage, of which I am capable, by the way. Years ago, there was a speaker, none of you will know him, his name is Charlie Jarvisen. Charlie talked about being on a diet and calling home and confessing to his wife that hanging out in the airport for three hours, waiting for a flight. He finally couldn't take it anymore. And he ate a Snickers bar. And she, of course, jumped all over him. And he said, you don't understand. This is a big victory, because I wanted all the Snickers bars. OK, so let's take that story and let's talk about that story for a second, because we're going to talk about the use and the power of story in sales copywriting. So that story could be used a number of different ways. It could be used as a bridge to talking about, excuse me, boy, this is worse for you than it is for me, but it isn't good for either one of us. I've heard that before. So I know it's early. Just help the person next to you. So that story could be used as a bridge to talk about failure environment versus success environment and why you need to create and be in a success environment. And then we could bridge from there to selling just about anything that had to do with being in a success environment. It could be used as a bridge to the discussion of the futility of willpower. And then if we were selling some sort of self-image reprogramming stuff, if we were selling subliminal tapes, if we were selling hypnagogic tapes, if we it would get us there, it could be used as a bridge to the importance of self-awareness. And if we were selling some sort of diagnostic process that made us more self-aware of our weaknesses and our strengths, we could use the story in order to bridge our way into that discussion. It could be used as a bridge to a discussion of what we allow inside our house, thereby bridging us to the principle of association. And by the way, I win the war. Most people win the war, just as an aside, at the perimeter of the house, not inside the house, fixing whatever has entered the house after it's in there is much more difficult than having a good perimeter. And basically what that story does is it gives us a bridge to any number of things that we might want to sell, which is one of the prime purposes of a story. And in this case, it creates rapport through shared experience. Because just about everybody is weak to something. So it gives us a shared experience that builds rapport. And the important thing to know about all of this is that stories are more powerful than statements. And so when you start to write copy to sell, it's important to think about whether at this particular place to make this particular point, we ought to be making an assertion, making a statement, or we ought to be telling a story. So here's some quick things about how that works. And then we'll talk about the kinds of stories you might want to use as an info marketer. So first of all, statement immediately manifests skepticism. People's guard goes up, and they essentially take the mental position of, I don't believe it, prove it. If we start with a story, curiosity is aroused, interest can occur, and skepticism is set aside. So something that you would be prone to believe, but still, if I said to you that it doesn't matter who gets elected, what the makeup of the Congress is, none of that matters. For as long as you live, no matter what, you will never, ever, ever, ever see government spending reduced, reigned in, controlled, or managed. You might even be prone to believe that, but still, never is a big word, isn't it? I mean, never, really, is it that futile? People will question that statement. So let me tell you a story. So Colin Powell was on the success events with us for a number of years. And I followed Colin, so we spent a lot of time in the green room together. And so one time, Colin comes in, and he's brought me a big, I don't know, about four times the size of a shoe box in circumference, and about the depth of a shoe box. And he says, here, I brought you some stuff. It's great, what stuff did you bring me? One of the box. And it's all the stuff that they give, every cabinet officer, by the way, every head of a department, every congressman, everybody gets stuff to give away to visitors and constituents. So there's cufflinks, there's tie-tacks, there's paper weights, there's pens, there's baseball caps, there's all kinds of stuff, clocks, wristwatches, you name it. And for each one of these people that has their seal of their office on it, so in his case, somewhere, there's a little secretary of state seal. And then their name is, their autograph is engraved on it somewhere. So here's stuff. So there's cufflinks and paper weights and pensets, and all with his signature and a little seal. I said, OK, cool. But why and why six of each thing? Why not one? He said, well, would you like to hear the story? I said, of course I'd like to hear the story. He said, the first week I'm in office, I'm in my office, I'm literally still putting stuff away. Door opens, guy comes in, and he's wheeling one of these big flatbed things, like they push stuff around on a Costco. And it's full to the brim with boxes. And he says, I say, what's that? And the guy says, this is your monthly supply, sir, of stuff you give away. And here's your pens, and here's your paper weights, and here's your cufflinks, and here's your tight-dacks, and here's your wristwatches, and here's your baseball caps, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. And he says, where am I going to? Oh, that's what that closet over there in the corner of your office is for. I stock it with this stuff for you. So I'll wheel over there, and I'll put it in there. He says, so next month, first week of the month, guy comes in the door, pushing the flatbed with boxes on it, piled as high as you can pile, and Colin says, what's that? He says, that's your monthly supply of the stuff you give away. He says, wait a minute. The closet's full. If I stood by the window and did nothing but throw it out, there's my job. I couldn't get rid of it. He says, don't bring me anymore until I, he says, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, sir. You don't understand. On the first of every month, I arrive with this push cart full of this stuff. Your job during the month is to give away everything that's in the closet. So there's room to put this stuff in when I arrive with it. I don't want it. You don't understand, sir, on the first of every month. So now I can assign a storage locker for it for you, sir. And I can take it down there, and I can put it in storage after you sign for it. But every month, I arrive with my cart full of stuff. So you've got to give it away. So Colin says, you know, I am the Secretary of State. He says, so I'm now aggravated. So I work my way up the ladder of procurement in the Pentagon because his office is under the Pentagon, understand everybody's under something different. But they're all getting loads of stuff. And he works his way all the way up to the Director of Procurement. And he says, look, he says, I don't want this stuff. I can't give it away. The guy says, you don't understand. He says, every month that guy's coming. They can put it in a storage locker. But he says, so now I'm aggravated. I'm really aggravated. So he says, I make a project now to finding out how much it costs. So he says, here, a pen set. How much do you think this costs? And you know, they look like cross pen and pencil. They're not cross, but they look like a cross pen and a cross pencil. And they got the little Secretary of State seal on it. And they got his name autographed on the side. And they're in a nice box with a little velvet thing in the back. And across the top of the box, his name's on it in gold. I said, I don't know. If you buy it at OfficeMax at retail, without your thing on it, I don't know, $30, $40, you know, tops, he says, we're paying $598 for every one of these. And he says, I get 144 of them every month. He says, so here's the best part of the story. He says, so a few months later, I'm still like stewing over this. And he says, we're in President Bush's office, me, Cheney, Rumsfeld. There's a bunch of us in there for a meeting. Meeting ends early. Everybody's kind of in a good mood, which is rare. And Bush says to everybody, hey, we got some time. Anybody got anything they want to talk about? And Colin says, yeah. He says, every month, they bring me this thing. And he says, I've been everywhere I can to try and put a stop to it. And they won't stop sending it. I can't stop it. And he says, the President Bush gets up. He walks across the Oval Office. He opens a big four-door closet. He says, I can't stop it either. Now, which is more persuasive to the point that we will never, ever, ever, ever, ever, ever, ever reign in government spending? The assertion or the story? The story is irrefutable. If the President of the United States can't stop the pens from coming, it truly is beyond all hope. So statement, skepticism, story, curiosity, interest, mine can only hold one emotionally trigger response at a time. If it is being curious and it is being interested, it can't be skeptical at the same time. It doesn't multitask. Very cool thing to know. So some of the stuff I showed you in session one, if you go back and you were to look at it again, here's a show you'll remember from the financial newsletter industry caught on videotape. What does the sales letter start with? It starts with a story. Dear subscriber, I don't consider myself a reporter. But recently, I flew up the East Coast to meet with a gentleman who's generated so much money, I arranged for a full-camera crew to record us at first to begin. And so the entire first half of this page is a story. Why? Because arouses curiosity, arouses interest, and sets aside skepticism. A statement can be challenged or differed with instantly. As soon as I make a statement, you can start to differ with it. You can start to disagree with it. If we are in disagreement, that's in the way to getting to agreement, which is what I need if I am going to sell you anything. If I start a story, we have been conditioned since childhood to pay attention to stories, to want to hear stories. Please, read me a story. We now have an app for that. There's a grandpa app. You just press it, hand the kid to pad, send them off in the corner, or it doesn't for you. I get it. But still, we have been conditioned since childhood. So statements are challenged. Stories are listened to. Statement can be confrontational. A story can be inspirational. If I say to you, you are fat, that's a confrontational statement. Most people do not like hearing it. It is not a good way to sell weight loss products. It has been tried, by the way, by many. And even with humor, it doesn't work. Halbert, who did a famous radio campaign that lasted a day of fat jokes for a weight loss clinic, not a good idea. You do get phone calls, but you don't get phone calls to book appointments. So the statement that you are fat, or you are fat and ugly, is not a welcome thing, but an inspirational story. So right now, as I left Cleveland's promo, because we have sweeps weeks right now, for the local news, is the local news anchor woman has been beefing up over the last couple of years. And it's gotten to the point of Oprah obviousness. The co-anchor, you can only see half of them if you have a small TV. And so it's tuned in to see how she has lost 40 pounds and changed her life. Over the next four nights, we're all going to see this. This will be a ratings hit, because it's an inspirational story. If it was all you fat folk, tune in. And we're going to tell you how to get the fat off. They would not tune in. So statements are confrontational. Stories can be inspirational. This is kind of cool. A statement requires authority. A story can even create authority. So if I tell you I have a miracle cure for cancer, you immediately start to think about, who is this guy, and why should I listen to him about cancer? Is he an MD? Is he at mail? Clinic? Is he this? Is he that? But if I tell you a story about having been diagnosed with cancer and being given three months to live and gone on a worldwide search for alternative healers who'd found a guy in a cave at the top of the mountain in Nepal who gave me the magic cancer curing formula, and I went on his diet. And to the shock and surprise of my doctors, when I went in three months later, all my cancer was gone. Your questions about, am I an MD? Do I practice at Mayo? Most of those questions are forgotten, because now you want to hear about the guy in Nepal and what the diet is. And I can probably sell you the grapefruit, chocolate, and bourbon cancer curing diet right behind it. Won't cure cancer, but you'll feel better between now and the time you die. By the way, it's risky to do cancer jokes. Statements can be copied and diluted. Personal story really can't be. Personal story is proprietary and protected. It's very hard these days to protect intellectual property. It's one of the adverse trends in this industry, is our fight to protect our content and to protect our sales content is harder and harder and harder and harder. The speed of knockoff is faster and faster and faster and faster, because the barrier to knockoff is lower and lower and lower and lower. There's a new information marketing infomercial. Just started airing, I think, this month or last month. I think it's called Brainiac or something like that. It's how to turn your kid into a whiz kid. It's sort of a reinvention of the hooked on phonics plus memory thing. If Howard Berg's here some way, Howard undoubtedly knows the story behind it. It's now what would have been a $399 product. It's now a $14.95 product. And then the upsells have to begin and the use of the list and all that. But anyway, it's a make your kid smarter, get better grades. Everybody will be able to memorize stuff. They do the magic math demonstration. None of this is new, but it may be new to you, because maybe you never saw it before. But it's not new. But it's very well done. It's a very effective show. But there is absolutely nothing in this that can't be knocked off at high speed, unless somebody had a great personal story. If the person behind the product had ADD and 14 other mental diseases and dyslexia and had been thrown out of 20 schools and was failing every class and found the magic formula now you would have something. So stories actually have equity. If you think about it, there are at least three you know, or should know, that have great equity. So Napoleon Hill's Andrew Carnegie story has great equity. If you take the Andrew Carnegie story out of Think and Grow Edge, if you take that story away from Napoleon Hill, what exactly do you have? Not much. You just got another success book written by a guy who went and interviewed some successful people and wrote a book about it. There's been thousands of those. They're all in a graveyard. Most of them lasted three to four months in the marketplace. Some of them have written by famous people. Think and Grow Rich has endured since 1937. Still sells hundreds of thousands of copies every year. Why? Because of the Andrew Carnegie story that people know and tell other people when they recommend the book, Maxwell Maltz story, for psychocyber-cybernetics, the only self-help book we know of to match 50 Shades of Gray in Numbers of Copy Soul. So the story of the woman who had surgery and couldn't see that her face had been improved, thereby the discovery of self-image, that story has equity. It was Max's and Max's alone. He was the plastic surgeon. She was his patient. No one can take that story. It's his and his alone. It's proprietary. It's unique and exclusive. Robert Kiyosaki, a more contemporary example. So Bob has made a living off of and a fortune from a single, relatively simple, personal story. And I like Bob, so no disrespect. But really, there's not a lot there but the story. And what's the story? Yeah, yell it out. I mean, yeah, two dads, rich dad, poor dad, observed the difference. That story has equity because it's his, you can't take it. So storytelling has real power in selling and it's an important part of copywriting. So one type of story, we'll talk about all the stories now that you need. So one type of story that's very important, we referenced it a little bit the other day, is the reason why story or the origin story. People want to know where you came from, how it is that you got to the point that you are standing in front of them, advising them, coercing them, presenting to them. How did you get there? Who the heck are you? Not business card, who are you? Not really resume, who are you? Who are you in the sense of being a human that I might identify with, feel empathy with, feel affinity for, somehow want to be associated with, learn from, hang out with, who are you? Why are you here? Now, we all know intellectually why everybody is here in front of us trying to sell us something. They're here for the money. Intellectually, pretty much you gotta have an IQ of a turnip not to know that whoever it is who has appeared in front of you, however they have done it, to sell you something, is there for the money. Intellectually, we know hardly anybody would be there if you took away the money. Go make 50 speeches this year and you don't get paid. Go make, knock on doors and do vacuum cleaner demonstrations, but you get no commission. So intellectually, we know that does not stop us from wanting to believe that there is another motive for the person being in front of us trying to sell us something that is more honorable, more, whoop, right, right, right, right. I heard a phone. Shall I be kind? Dwight said this time, that's two. Two. Amishman, Mule, and wife. If you haven't heard the joke, ask somebody on the break. Now, we're gonna have to go back to the room and have a break, okay? Next time, he kills somebody. Okay, so they want to believe there's a reason you're there other than money. Therefore, it's important to give them that reason. It's helpful if it's legit, if you do have a second authentic reason for doing what it is that you're doing, but whether it's legit or not, I'll tell you it is important for you to have it. Last, they want to know, how can I feel it's okay to trust this person? I, um, we, as you know, there's a book, most of you now have it, about trust-based marketing. We grossly underestimate how scary we are to other people. We grossly underestimate it. You know, you stand in front of the mirror, you're ready to go downstairs, you, kind of. I mean, it ain't Brad Pitt looking back at me, but I don't expect little children to run screaming into the bushes, either. I don't feel I'm really scary. So one of the things I do at these events, when you don't see me, I'm not sleeping. I'm having private appointments with titanium members, platinum members, clients, et cetera. And so yesterday at an appointment, I won't identify her, but at an appointment, a woman business person who had a private appointment with me, and it's the first time we've had a private appointment. Immediately at the start of the appointment said, I'm a little uncomfortable being in a hotel room with you alone, because the last time I did that with a speaker, he attempted to freshen my breath, but not with an altoid. Now, she told me that why. Because she was genuinely anxious about the circumstance, and that was her way of issuing a warning, should I be so inclined. Now, I don't think of myself in that context at all. I was a little surprised. She thought of me in that context. However, however, I thought about a little later. Yeah, I was sort of flattering. Hey, at this age, anybody thinking you can rape them is, you know. But I mean, now think about this for a second. We already have a relationship. It's not like this is off the street. It's not like we're strangers in a sense. This person's a member. She comes to functions. She reads me every month. Still, that's there. We grossly underestimate how scary we are to other people. So the last thing they want to know is how can I feel it's okay to trust this person? So the origin story is pretty important. Generally speaking, people don't make sense without their origin stories. The Lone Ranger makes no sense. Batman makes no sense. The new green arrow on the CW makes no sense without the origin story. The typical infomarketing origin stories, the most common are these. I found the key and I feel guilty keeping it to myself, which also provides the extra reason why. I had to figure it out for myself the really hard way and I'm still mad at all the people who misled me, all the people who sent me down a blind alley and the best revenge is now sharing what I figured out with you. Third kind of story is this whole thing, teaching, training, writing. It sort of found me because people kept asking as they found out how successful I was at EMU training. And so all the other EMU trainers like you kept asking me and so I finally felt compelled to develop a sensible way to answer the question. And for the outsider coming into a niche, my friend, friends in the field, the thing that I was the marketing genius of the free world begged and begged and begged me to help them. And so I took a look at plumbers and I realized plumbers didn't know anything about marketing and I realized I could change the industry and that's why I'm here. Those are your most common infomarketer origin stories. They work, by the way, they're fine, said a little more elegantly than I just said them. So keep this in mind about that type of story. People are vaguely, unconsciously uncomfortable at bare minimum when a good origin slash reason why story is missing. Even if they can't enunciate it, even if it doesn't directly and consciously bother them, it unconsciously bothers them. There's something wrong. They don't really know what's wrong. But again, if you think of it in the Batman context, if we present Batman to people without an origin story, we have presented something very, very different than if we present him with the origin story. Every time that franchise is reinvented in the movies, which has been five times in my lifetime, the origin story must be told. Every time in the graphic novels, a new series is created, the origin story must be told because otherwise he's just a big, dumb, scary, frightening guy in a goofy suit running around beating the crap out of everybody. This is not a good thing as soon as we put the origin story. People are profoundly interested in the origin story. They are fascinated with why you are what you are where you are. It should incidentally make no difference. I should be able to tell you there's absolutely no reason whatsoever for me being here in front of you, but I want as much of your money as I can possibly get my damn hands on. That should make no difference. What I deliver to you is either valuable or not valuable. It either makes you money or doesn't make you money. My motive should not matter intellectually, and some of you would be able to get past it intellectually, but not internally. We are often guilty of not telling the origin story, thinking everybody knows it, or we are bored with it. I too am guilty, so it's something to be careful of. Other kinds of stories that you will need has an infomarketer. You need good stories about your champions and their journeys. A lot of people shortcut their testimonials and their champion stories by telling the end. The after is no good without the before, and it is the drama between the two that really matters. It is the journey that really matters. How bad was it? How good is it? What was the journey? So we have to be good at telling those stories. About your own miracles. You have to be good at describing the challenges you met, or writing that so that it is told in someone else's voice. So of the people you saw up here at the very start, the niche infomarketers, the person who is, for whatever reason, best at this is Donna Cratch. You can wake Donna up in the middle of the night and she starts telling you about not being able to feed her baby. She just goes into that automatically. Orrant has a story, but he has to be prompted to tell it, and he doesn't really like telling it. Sometimes he thinks everybody is right. Donna meets somebody at a cocktail party and they say, what do you do? She starts with, I couldn't feed my baby. That's where she starts. After a while, I can now tell you that I couldn't feed my baby's story. But she's right to be doing that. So you have to be able to tell about your own miracles. You have to be able to tell about your own adventures because one of the things that is true about this business is customers who hang around for a long period of time. What we call lifers. Are there less and less and less for what Gary Halbert called the clicks on the dial? So the clicks on the dial are the things that we have to teach you. The things that you have to teach whoever it is that you teach. I only got so many clicks on the dial. John's only got so many clicks on the dial. We just spin the dial where it stops and tell you that again. And that's okay for a year, two years, three years, but ten years. Everybody knows the clicks on my dial if they've been around me for ten years. So they're not hanging around for that anymore. They're hanging around for all sorts of other reasons. That's why really info marketing, it's a convenient way to describe what we do. It's not a particularly accurate way to describe what we do. The business is more complex than that. And so one of the things people do is they live vicariously through your adventures. Through the places you go, the things you do, the people you meet. So why is a picture of you with Adam West and the Batmobile so valuable? It's valuable because you get to turn around and use it with your herd who didn't have that experience, but thinks that experience is cool. That's why. But you've got to create a story about that. So like we had Gene Simmons from KISS at the Super Conference, the smartest guy. The smartest guy. Now it was funny. I don't know if he did it because he was smart or he did it just to be funny, but the smartest guy of everybody who came to get their picture taken with Gene is the guy who brought the shoebox of KISS 8-track tapes to be autographed. Now I think that's funny, but see it gave him a story. Everybody else just got a picture. He's got a picture and a story. So you want to be able to tell stories about your adventures. And by the way, you may not think your adventures are much. Compare yours to the way most people live their lives. Your adventures are significant. You want to be able to tell stories about you that reveal character and strength. You want to be able to tell stories about you that reveal humanity. So people can believe, if he can, I can. If she can, I can. It's an important part of this business. We also grossly underestimate how little self-confidence people have. Even people who have eight years of college, even people who have professional practices, even people who have some measure of success, we grossly underestimate how little self-confidence they have about doing anything new and different. And therefore, they have to borrow ours. And you have to be good at telling stories that arouse curiosity and create mystique. The way you decide what stories you need and which to use, well, one answer is objections that need erased. What are all of the reasons why somebody might not accept your proposition? Might not respond as you wish, might not buy. We need a good story for every one of those objections. Assumptions or negative opinions about you or your industry that need corrected. What's a good, we need a good story for that. So right now, in the financial category that I showed you, and now in the direct selling network marketing category, everybody's using Warren Buffett. It's very hard to find a, here's an investment strategy pitch without somebody somehow tying it to it being the strategy Warren Buffett is really using. Why? Because everybody knows Buffett. Network marketing industry loves to tell everybody that Warren Buffett, Berkshire Hathaway, now owns Pampercheff. Why did they buy Pampercheff? For the same reasons that you should join XYZ Drink Company. Now, the connection's pretty, but it's enough if you tell that story well. Assertions that need proven, particularly those that can't be proven scientifically, clinically, or factually. Then we need stories that prove them to the person in a philosophical position. So some elements that make for a great story. One is the power of a punchline or a twist at the end. So one of the ones I just saw very recently when I do with the book now. I had a book, here we go. So power of a punchline or a twist at the end. Horrible to read to you from the stage, but I'm going to do it. So this is one of my titanium members. This is, I identified him. Where are you now Nelson? Our pastor and residence. Are you in the room Nelson? There you are. Where? Where, where, there he is. Okay, so if any of you need counseling, well, if any of you want counseling, there's no question of need, right? So here's one in Nelson's books, The Greatness Principle, okay? So I will read you a parable. It's not very long, fortunately. So you may have heard the old story about the college senior. Now that's an interesting way to start a story because it means he stole it. You may have heard the old story about the college senior, let's call him Justin, who had his eye on a bright red corvette at a dealership close to his parents' house. In the weeks leading up to his graduation, Justin dropped subtle and not so subtle hints to his dad that he wanted the corvette as his graduation present. After all, his parents were wealthy and there was no reason they couldn't buy it for him. The morning of graduation, Justin woke up thinking about the corvette. He could hardly concentrate on the commencement ceremony. His mind was already flying down the highway in his new car. After the diplomas have been passed out, Justin's parents hosted a blowout graduation party for him at their house. Finally, the moment came when Justin's dad tapped him on the shoulder and asked him to join him in the den. Justin knew this was it. He was about to get exactly what he wanted. Once they were seated in the den, Justin's dad handed him a package. He unwrapped it to find a beautifully bound Bible inscribed with his name. Disappointed and angry, Justin pointed a finger at his father and said, with all your money you give me a Bible, you knew I wanted that car. Then he stormed out of the house leaving the book behind. Years later, when Justin had become a successful businessman himself, he got a call that his dad was sick. Justin's explosion on graduation day had caused a rift in the relationship and he hadn't seen his father since. When he got the phone call, though, he decided he should go home and mend things. Unfortunately, before Justin could get there, he received another call saying that his dad had passed away and that everything had been willed to him. When Justin finally got to his parents' house to take care of what he needed, taking care of, he was full of sadness and regret. As he was going through some books and papers in the den, he found the still new Bible from his graduation day. He couldn't hold back the tears as he opened the Bible and started turning the pages. Suddenly, a car key dropped from an envelope. Did you hear the bones? Suddenly, a car key dropped from an envelope that had been taped to the Bible's back cover. By the way, when you put a CD in a book, put it in the front. On the tip, don't interrupt an emotional story with a punchline with humor. On the back of the key ring, Justin's father had written, Congratulations, son, celebrate with a new Corvette. Powerful story. Now, we can do a lot with that story where he chose to took it, take it. You and I aren't that different from Justin. While he may have been spoiled and overly demanding of his parents, he did something that we were prone to do more often than we realize. He completely missed the gift. His father, remember this is a religious, his father wanted to give him because it wasn't packaged in the way he thought it should be. Et cetera, et cetera, I won't read you the rest of it. You should applaud. Now, so that's the power of a story with a twist at the end. That's the power of a story that gets a gasp, a sink in the stomach, a visceral emotional reaction at a key point that then leads you to using the parable to demonstrate whatever it is that you built the parable to do. It's important to understand that stories can be reverse engineered from the point you want them to make. You got to know what it is you want to drive home and then you go and look, search for or create the story. Second kind of story that works is the self deprecating humor story. They have to be careful not to use too many of them or you wound your status with your audience. You can't use too many in succession like in the same sales letter. There's usually only room for one. In the same sales speech, there's usually only room for one or you start to diminish your status. But one is okay. So if Colombo wanted to, he could use the story that I told you. He could stop short of Bush because really the story if you stop short of the Oval Office is an ancient story. Every speaker has used about being at the head table at a banquet and the person comes along who's dispensing the butter and puts one pat of butter in front of everybody. And the speaker who is there to be honored asks for two pats of butter and the guy says, no sir. Everybody only gets one pat of butter. The guy says, you apparently don't know who I am. I'm the Secretary of State at which point the guy says, and I'm the guy in charge of the butter. Now, that's the same story. You understand? Colombo's story with the guy pushing the cart is the pat of butter story which dates back to the beginning of butter and banquets. And he could stop it there and use it as a self deprecating humor story if he wanted to. The cufflink story. You guys all know the cufflink story? How many don't know the cufflink story? Oh really? Okay, this is not original. It did happen to me, but it's happened to other dumb people too. Now, I understand I just did the same thing Nelson did. In case you've heard it from somebody else, I just covered it. You get it? So it did happen to me though. So I'll tell you the way it happened to me. So I'm speaking. I'm on the road. I always wear French cuff shirts when I speak. And so I'm in some rat hole town in, I don't know, Alabama, Tolessons. I'm in some, we have some folks here from Alabama. They're easy to find. They look like the offensive line of Alabama. You'll spot them back there. As I said last night, I haven't seen them in a while and I said, well at least we know they haven't run out of barbecue in Alabama boys. So I'm in this rat hole town in Alabama in this crappy motel and I'm going to go speak the next morning and I'm laying out my clothes and I go into my Dopp kit for my cuff links. And I have no cuff links. I have left the cuff links at home. I'm sure I have no cuff links. And all my stage suits are cut short to show the cuff. So it's not like I can use a paper clip, you know, because it's good to show. And I'm aggravated and so I go downstairs and ask where the best, you know, men's wear store is in town that might still be open that I can go get some cuff links and the person at the front desk tells me Sears. Is the best men's wear store in town. So I get a cab and I go over to Sears and I go into men's wear department to get cuff links and of course they have no cuff links and so, well, the Kmart might have, so I go over to Kmart. So I go to Kmart. Of course I'm getting more aggravated and, you know, worked up as I go from store to store to store. None of them having any cuff links in this rat hole town. And so by the time I get to the last place, which I forget, it was either Walmart or Kmart, I am, you know, I'm not a calm person anyway. So I'm one step away from letting somebody have it. And when this poor young girl with the tooth is putting words together to explain to me that no sir, we don't have cuff links, but of course I don't let her finish her sentence because I'm, so I now give her the 15 minutes of what a rat hole place this is and how everybody's a moron and how can there be a city without a single friggin cuff link in it and what the hell is wrong with you people anyway, you know. And so, and how important I am and I'm speaking the next morning and I've got to have cuff links because I have French cuff shirts and my jacket is, and I've got to have cuff links and there have to be, she, I'm finally done, you know, she says, well sir, we, I'm sorry, we don't, we just don't have cuff links, she says, but sir, we have a lot of nice shirts with buttons on them. Hmm, that thought never occurred to me. Has a way to solve this problem. Now, so, so that story, right, is a self-deprecating story. It's a, the thing you use it for mostly is a bridge to how we get myopia. We get narrow focused on one solution to one thing and we can't, we can't think creatively. You can use it then to sell mastermind. It's a reason why you should be in platinum. It's a reason why you should be in peak performers. It's a perfect bridge to that because left to our own devices, we get so narrow focused on one solution and only one solution to one problem and that solution can't work for us. Therefore, there is no solution to the problem and that's why you want to be in a group of other people who stimulate other, see, see, see, you see how we, we can use it. Okay, so self-deprecating, good humor and of course you want vivid detail. Classic story plots most useful to info marketers. One is the Charles Atlas, Rocky, they all laughed when I sat down at the piano until I started to play. The classic plot line of being underestimated, being thought of as the least likely to succeed, being the guy who was saying, kicked in his face by the bully who now through some mechanism, be it the exercise device, the money making miracle, the whatever rises above all of them and now has the last laugh. That's a plot line that works in the info marketing industry again and again and again. Worked for Charles Atlas, works in selling fitness today. The little engine who could, which proves you can too, story. So that's the person who is really least likely to succeed. The one-legged golfer's almost in that category, right? But the really, really, really, really poor person, the really, really, really, really dumb person. And so the story doesn't have to be about you, remember? You might have been poor, I'm not suggesting any of you are dumb. Although, yeah, I told you I was supposed to be a social worker. I was also suggested I might be a coroner, in which case cause of death, fat and dumb. Next. So it doesn't have to be you. It can be one of your champions about which you have this fabulous little engine that could story. And that sells things because people need to believe they can too. And when they see somebody who is much more disadvantaged than they are, a blackie story. If you don't know what a blackie story is, I don't have time to give you the whole history this morning. I'm sure you can Google it and find it. But the Andrew Carnegie Napoleon Hill story is a blackie story. It's a story where there is someone. The Kenny Rogers song, The Gambler, with the guy on the train, the old, wise, that's a blackie story. There's some old, grizzled possessor of a secret, some dying wizard who with his last breath passes along. Someone who opens doors that otherwise would not have been possible to have been opened. So Napoleon Hill used a blackie story with Andrew Carnegie. There is the Eureka story of the sudden realization. The light bulb finally went off. And there is the Eureka story. There is the inspired by story that you were motivated to become what it is that you are because now of some persuasive story that makes a point about what you are going to sell. And of course there are the lost secrets, the hidden secrets, the amazing discovery story. Which again, if you look at the financial stuff I showed you earlier to show you the code, you will also find these stories used liberally by those marketers. So the little engine who could, who proves you can too, remember I showed you the IRS worker who turns $1842 into a million. A little guy doing big things. The hidden secrets I showed you, George Bush's secret retirement legacy. So you will find these stories almost everywhere that you look being used by good info marketers in almost every category. So if you want to make more money, you will tell more stories and you will tell stories better. The more depth you become at storytelling, the more you build up your bank of good stories that can be tweaked and used for different purposes as you need them, as I showed you at the beginning of our time together this morning. And the better you are at telling them, the more money you will make.
